flashcards.front: Define a Walk in graph theory.
flashcards.back: A sequence of vertices and edges where each edge's endpoints are the vertices immediately preceding and following it in the sequence. Vertices and edges can be repeated.

flashcards.front: What is the key difference between a Trail and a Path?
flashcards.back: A trail forbids repeated edges. A path forbids repeated vertices (which also means no repeated edges). All paths are trails, but not all trails are paths.
A circuit is a closed trail. It is a walk that starts and ends at the same vertex and has no repeated edges.

flashcards.front: How is the connectivity of an undirected graph defined?
flashcards.back: An undirected graph is connected if and only if there is a path between every pair of distinct vertices.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between Walks, Trails, and Paths?
flashcards.back: All paths are trails, and all trails are walks. The set of paths is a subset of the set of trails, which is a subset of the set of walks.
